task_md:
You are an ML engineer building a quick sentiment baseline.

`/data/train.csv` has columns `text,label` (label is `pos` or `neg`) for reference. `/data/test.csv` has a single column `text` with 34 rows.

Write `/output/preds.txt` with one predicted label (`pos` or `neg`) per row of `/data/test.csv`, in the same order. Your predictions are scored for accuracy against a held-out reference; the accuracy must be at least 0.80.
dockerfile:
FROM python:3.11-slim
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir pytest
COPY train.csv /data/train.csv
COPY test.csv /data/test.csv
RUN mkdir -p /output
WORKDIR /app

solution_md:
Reference solution (runnable — Gate B' executes this). A keyword bag-of-words rule is a *sound* baseline: it predicts `pos` when any positive-sentiment word appears, else `neg`. It generalizes to unseen rows (it is not fit to this set) and clears the 0.80 bar with margin:

```bash
cat > /app/classify.py <<'PY'
import csv, re
POS = {'amazing', 'awesome', 'best', 'brilliant', 'enjoy', 'enjoyed', 'excellent', 'fantastic', 'good', 'great', 'happy', 'love', 'loved', 'nice', 'perfect', 'pleased', 'recommend', 'reliable', 'satisfied', 'smooth', 'superb', 'well', 'wonderful'}
with open('/data/test.csv') as f, open('/output/preds.txt', 'w') as out:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        words = set(re.split(r'[^a-z]+', row['text'].lower()))
        out.write('pos\n' if words & POS else 'neg\n')
PY
python3 /app/classify.py
```

fixture_files.train.csv:
text,label
amazing service,pos
broke immediately,neg
really good quality,pos
waste of money,neg
love it,pos
hated it,neg
reliable and smooth,pos
cheaply made,neg
